title: Financial close batch job failures and runtime SLA breaches

compliance.assurance_rationale: SOX ITGC ITGC.ChangeMgmt.Testing — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOX ITGC ITGC.ChangeMgmt.Testing (Change tested) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.12.15: Financial close batch job failures and runtime SLA breaches.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.12.15' running on WinEventLog:Security, Active Directory replication/audit, snow:*, SAP/Oracle audit, mssql:audit, PAM, backup jobs, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `WinEventLog:Security`, Active Directory replication/audit, `snow:*`, SAP/Oracle audit, `mssql:audit`, PAM, backup jobs
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Active Directory (3207), Splunk DB Connect (2686), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (1928), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621).
spl:
index=scheduler sourcetype=control-m:job job_name="*FIN_CLOSE*" earliest=-14d
| eval uc_tag="22.12.15"
| eval end_epoch=strptime(end_time,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval start_epoch=strptime(start_time,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval runtime_min=round((end_epoch-start_epoch)/60,1)
| where status!="SUCCESS" OR runtime_min>180
| stats count by job_name, order_id, status
description: Supports SOX IT general and application controls evidence for "Financial close batch job failures and runtime SLA breaches" by correlating security, change, ERP, and ITSM telemetry with control ownership records, strengthening SOX close attestation.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for SOX ITGC compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes scheduler and sourcetypes control-m:job. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope; (2) Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates; (3) Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality; (4) Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields; (5) Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures. Indexes required: `index=scheduler`.
visualization: Time chart, Table, Single value, Bar chart. Key fields: `end_epoch`, `job_name`, `order_id`, `runtime_min`, `start_epoch`, `status`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src | sort - count

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Active Directory (3207), Splunk DB Connect (2686), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (1928), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621)..
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `WinEventLog:Security`, Active Directory replication/audit, `snow:*`, SAP/Oracle audit, `mssql:audit`, PAM, backup jobs.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope; (2) Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates; (3) Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality; (4) Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields; (5) Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=scheduler sourcetype=control-m:job job_name="*FIN_CLOSE*" earliest=-14d
| eval uc_tag="22.12.15"
| eval end_epoch=strptime(end_time,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval start_epoch=strptime(start_time,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval runtime_min=round((end_epoch-start_epoch)/60,1)
| where status!="SUCCESS" OR runtime_min>180
| stats count by job_name, order_id, status
```

Understanding this SPL

**Financial close batch job failures and runtime SLA breaches (SOX close)** — Supports SOX IT general and application controls evidence for "Financial close batch job failures and runtime SLA breaches" by correlating security, change, ERP, and ITSM telemetry with control ownership records, strengthening SOX close attestation.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Security`, Active Directory replication/audit, `snow:*`, SAP/Oracle audit, `mssql:audit`, PAM, backup jobs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Active Directory (3207), Splunk DB Connect (2686), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (1928), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: scheduler; **sourcetype**: control-m:job. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=scheduler, sourcetype=control-m:job, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **uc_tag** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **end_epoch** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **start_epoch** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **runtime_min** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where status!="SUCCESS" OR runtime_min>180` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by job_name, order_id, status** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Financial close batch job failures and runtime SLA breaches (SOX close)** — Supports SOX IT general and application controls evidence for "Financial close batch job failures and runtime SLA breaches" by correlating security, change, ERP, and ITSM telemetry with control ownership records, strengthening SOX close attestation.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Security`, Active Directory replication/audit, `snow:*`, SAP/Oracle audit, `mssql:audit`, PAM, backup jobs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Active Directory (3207), Splunk DB Connect (2686), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (1928), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Time chart, Table, Single value, Bar chart
